title: Clearing the Path / Notes on Dhamma — Ñāṇavīra Thera
subtitle: A collection of letters and philosophical essays on the Buddha's teaching, centering on Fundamental Structure — a phenomenological structural analysis of experience as dynamic conditionality

summary.one_line: Ñāṇavīra's Clearing the Path (1960-1965) develops a 'Fundamental Structure' of experience based on dependent arising (paṭicca-samuppāda), refracted through Kierkegaardian existentialism and Eddingt...
summary.scope: Covers the Fundamental Structure essay, the letters on personality-view (sakkāya-diṭṭhi), the analysis of dukkha, dependent arising, the concept of a being (satta), the tetrahedron diagram of intentional experience, and the Kierkegaardian method of negative thinking.

sources.label: Ñāṇavīra Thera — Clearing the Path / Notes on Dhamma (1960-1965). PDF: ctp_book_v1.pdf in corpus root. 15,392 readable lines. Full text available.

body.text: Ñāṇavīra's Fundamental Structure is a diagram (derived from Eddington's quantum diagrams) that maps the relations between intention, responsibility, context, conditionality, and hunger (taṇhā) that constitute experience. The diagram is a tetrahedron: four vertices (subject, object, intention, act) with six edges representing the relations between them. This IS the constraint space (S, C, A) approached from the phenomenological side. S is the space of possible experience, C is the structure of conditionality (paṭicca-samuppāda), A is nibbāna as the cessation of the dynamic. The tetrahedron IS the simplest positive geometry — the minimal complete structure from which all experience is generated.

'Positive or abstract thinking abstracts from existence and is thus incapable of thinking it continuously.' Ñāṇavīra's method is negative thinking — not asserting what reality IS but analyzing the CONDITIONS under which anything appears. This IS the Tantraloka's apophatic tradition: the 38th principle (Anākhyā) is the Undefinable, known only by what it is NOT. Ñāṇavīra's negative thinking IS the method of approaching the 38th — removing false positives until only the silence remains. His quote from Kierkegaard: 'Precisely because the negative is present in existence, and present everywhere (for existence is a constant process of becoming), it is necessary to become aware of its presence continuously, as the only safeguard against it.'

Ñāṇavīra's analysis of satta (a being): 'Dukkham eva hi sambhoti, dukkhaṃ tiṭṭhati veti ca' — only suffering arises, only suffering ceases. The 'being' (satta) is not a substance but a dynamic configuration of the five aggregates (pañc'upādānakkhandhā). This IS the jīva (the individuated configuration) in the Tantraloka. The error of personality-view (sakkāya-diṭṭhi) IS the Sv(O) error — taking the configuration as self-grounding. Ñāṇavīra's solution: the sotāpanna (stream-enterer) has abandoned sakkāya-diṭṭhi — they no longer identify the configuration as a self. This IS partial recognition (Rec) — knowing that the configuration is empty of self-nature. Full recognition (FullRec) would be the arahant — the configuration that has entirely ceased the error.

gaps: Full extraction of the Fundamental Structure essay; the tetrahedron diagram is referenced but the actual figure is not extractable from the PDF

issues.title: Diagram extraction
issues.description: The Fundamental Structure diagram (tetrahedron) cannot be extracted from the PDF. It would need to be reconstructed from the textual description.

versions.summary: Initial RO for Ñāṇavīra's Clearing the Path. 3 passages mapping Fundamental Structure to constraint space, negative thinking to apophatic method, satta to configuration.

timeline.event: RO created. Ñāṇavīra's Fundamental Structure is now formally mapped to the constraint space framework.
